text.fullText: Re: Docket ID EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Proposed Revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Regulations Submitted by: Raritan Headwaters Association Bedminster, New Jersey --- Introduction Raritan Headwaters Association (RHA) respectfully submits the following comments regarding EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulations. RHA is a nationally accredited land trust and watershed organization dedicated to protecting water quality and drinking water supplies in the 470-square-mile Upper Raritan River watershed in New Jersey. Much of our watershed lies within the region protected under the Highlands Water Protection and Planning Act, enacted by the State of New Jersey to safeguard drinking water resources that serve millions of residents. The Upper Raritan watershed supplies drinking water locally and contributes to water resources serving roughly 70 percent of New Jersey’s population downstream. Protecting these headwaters is fundamentally about infrastructure reliability, fiscal responsibility, and long-term water supply stability. Section 401 is a central feature of the Clean Water Act’s cooperative federalism structure. Congress deliberately preserved state authority to determine whether federally permitted activities comply with state water quality standards. Any revision that constrains that authority must be carefully evaluated against the statute’s text and purpose. --- I. Section 401 Reflects Congressional Intent to Preserve State Authority Section 401 requires state certification that a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with enumerated provisions of the Act, including state water quality standards adopted pursuant to Section 303. Those standards include: · Designated uses · Numeric and narrative criteria · State anti-degradation policies Compliance with these standards frequently requires evaluating the effects of the permitted activity — not merely the characteristics of a discrete discharge point. Hydrology, groundwater interaction, sediment transport, and downstream effects are directly relevant to whether state water quality standards are met. A regulatory interpretation that limits review to a narrow “discharge-only” inquiry risks substituting federal administrative constraint for the broader role Congress assigned to states. Such a shift would alter the balance of authority established by statute. If Congress had intended certification to apply only to the physical attributes of a discharge pipe, it could have said so explicitly. Instead, Section 401 links certification to compliance with state water quality standards, standards that are inherently watershed-based. --- II. Drinking Water Infrastructure and Fiscal Responsibility in the Highlands The New Jersey Highlands region was specifically protected by state law because it supplies drinking water to a substantial portion of the state. Reservoirs such as Spruce Run Reservoir and Round Valley Reservoir depend on stable headwater flows and protected recharge areas. In New Jersey’s Highlands, protecting drinking water starts in the headwaters. If Section 401 review is limited to a single discharge pipe instead of the full project and its watershed impacts, we risk missing the bigger picture; groundwater drawdown, baseflow reduction, and cumulative stress on reservoirs like Spruce Run and Round Valley. Once source water is degraded, treatment costs rise and drought resilience declines. From a fiscal standpoint, preventing upstream degradation reduces: · Long-term treatment costs · Reservoir dredging and sediment management expenses · Infrastructure upgrades borne by ratepayers · Emergency response costs during drought Watersheds function as integrated systems. Scientific consensus and decades of federal and state practice recognize that water quality is influenced not only by discrete discharge points, but also by land disturbance, hydrologic alteration, groundwater interaction, and cumulative watershed impacts. A certification process that does not allow states to consider these interconnected factors risks overlooking impacts that materially affect water quality and designated uses. Section 401 serves as a preventative safeguard. Weakening its practical application could shift costs downstream to utilities and taxpayers. --- III. Anti-Degradation Standards Are Core State Responsibility New Jersey has designated numerous streams in the Upper Raritan watershed as Category One (C1) waters due to their exceptional ecological and water supply value. State anti-degradation policies require maintenance of existing water quality in these waters. Anti-degradation analysis often requires evaluation of cumulative and indirect impacts. If certification review is artificially limited, states may be constrained in applying their own standards as authorized under Section 303. Section 401 should continue to function as Congress intended: as a mechanism through which states ensure that federally permitted activities comply with state water quality standards in full. --- IV. Regulatory Certainty and State Expertise We recognize the importance of regulatory clarity and timely permitting decisions. However, clarity should not come at the expense of statutory balance. States are closest to: · Local hydrologic data · Groundwater recharge dynamics · Drinking water infrastructure · Watershed-specific vulnerabilities Preserving meaningful state review authority under Section 401 does not create uncertainty; rather, it ensures that certification decisions are grounded in site-specific knowledge and consistent with established state standards. Clear guidance confirming that states may evaluate the permitted activity where there is a reasonable nexus to water quality would provide both predictability and statutory fidelity. As an organization engaged daily in watershed restoration, dam removal, land conservation, and water quality monitoring, RHA sees firsthand how incremental upstream impacts affect downstream communities and drinking water infrastructure. --- V. Recommendations We respectfully urge EPA to ensure that the final rule: 1. Preserves state authority to evaluate the permitted activity as necessary to determine compliance with state water quality standards. 2. Confirms that anti-degradation policies remain enforceable through Section 401 certification. 3. Maintains the cooperative federalism structure Congress established. 4. Provides regulatory clarity without narrowing state authority beyond what the statute requires. --- Conclusion The Upper Raritan watershed demonstrates a straightforward principle: protecting headwaters protects downstream communities and infrastructure. Section 401 was crafted by Congress to empower states as partners in protecting water quality. Any revision should respect that statutory structure, preserve state authority, and ensure continued protection of drinking water resources that serve millions of Americans. We appreciate EPA’s consideration of these comments. Respectfully submitted, Mara Tippett Executive Director Raritan Headwaters Association
